food_subgroup: Burmese dry-style flat rice noodles with minced pork tomato sauce and peanut oil dressing

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Shan Noodles, approximately 450g, consisting of 350g noodles with sauce and 100ml broth on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Shan Noodles — FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 + component calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Table 2020 for similar flat rice noodle dishes and component calculations using USDA SR Legacy for minced pork, tomato sauce, and peanut oil.

gi_source.title: GI ~62 — flat rice noodles (khauk swè) are similar in composition to kway teow. Flat rice noodles have a medium GI.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI estimated based on similar flat rice noodles (kway teow) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The peanut oil fat dressing provides partial GI moderation. The minced pork (~18g protein) helps moderate postprandial glucose.

hypertension.guidance: With ~800mg sodium per bowl (40% of daily limit), this dish can be managed as a main meal if other daily sodium intake is controlled. Opting not to consume the side broth can reduce sodium to ~650mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from flat rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Due to a GI of ~62 and a GL of ~33.5, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion (e.g., 250g instead of 350g) to lower the glycemic load below 25. The peanut oil dressing should not be reduced as it helps moderate GI.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate sodium and saturated fat. While peanut oil and sesame provide beneficial fats and antioxidants, overall intake should be considered within a balanced diet to manage cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Peanut oil (MUFA-rich), sesame seeds (lignan sesamin), and tomato (lycopene) contribute anti-inflammatory compounds, making this dish potentially suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shan Noodles are a traditional Burmese flat rice noodle dish originating from the Shan plateau in eastern Myanmar. This dish features silky flat rice noodles tossed in a savory sauce made with minced pork, tomato, and soy, then dressed with peanut oil and sesame seeds. It is typically served dry, with a small bowl of clear broth on the side. Per bowl (~450g), it provides approximately 450 kcal, 18g protein, 16g fat, and 54g carbohydrate. With a GI of ~62 and GL of ~33.5, it is considered medium to high glycemic, primarily due to the rice noodles. Sodium content is around 800mg, which can be reduced to 650mg if the side broth is not consumed, making it lower in sodium than many hot noodle soups. The flavor profile is lighter, non-spicy, and characterized by tomato-soy notes. It is available at Burmese restaurants, such as Shan Kitchen in Golden Mile Complex, Singapore. The dish is dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is adapted from FAO/INFOODS for similar dishes and component calculations using USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall confidence is medium due to the need for component calculation and analogy.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed data for these specific components in this prepared dish were not available in the primary sources (FAO/INFOODS, USDA SR Legacy) used for calculation. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this dish type per USDA SR Legacy component data).
